skill.name: swift-concurrency
skill.description: Guidance for applying Swift Concurrency patterns, including async/await, actors, tasks, Sendable, testing, Core Data, performance, and Swift 6 migration.
skill.summary: Apply Swift Concurrency patterns safely across app code, tests, Core Data, and Swift 6 migrations.
skill.author: AvdLee

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, storage, reconnaissance, and weak-crypto patterns. Manual review found these are markdown code fences, Swift code examples, course links, and Swift terminology, with no executable scripts or prompt injection evidence. The skill is publishable with low risk because it is documentation-heavy and includes external educational links and sample network code.

low_findings.title: Command execution detections are markdown false positives
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged many backticks as Ruby or shell execution. The reviewed locations are markdown inline code and Swift code fences that describe concurrency APIs, not executable skill behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is markdown documentation containing Swift examples and inline API names. No executable script files or command invocation logic were found in the reviewed skill structure.

low_findings.title: Network detections are educational links and sample code
low_findings.description: The network hits point to course links, package references, and Swift URLSession examples. They do not cause the skill itself to make network requests or exfiltrate data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed URLs are visible references or placeholder example endpoints. They are not hidden destinations and are not paired with credential access or automatic execution.

low_findings.title: Browser storage detection is a Swift testing example
low_findings.description: The sensitive storage hit is a TaskLocal database example in Swift Testing documentation. It is not browser local storage, cookie access, or credential persistence.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact lines define a Swift @TaskLocal value for test scoping. This is unrelated to browser storage APIs and does not access user secrets.

low_findings.title: Blocker categories map to Swift terminology, not malicious behavior
low_findings.description: The weak-crypto and reconnaissance labels appear to match unrelated Swift text such as @unchecked Sendable guidance, migration prose, Instruments checks, or fetch examples. No evidence of cryptographic misuse, host reconnaissance, or network scanning was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static categories do not match the semantic context of the reviewed lines. Confidence is high because the files are educational markdown, but the scanner output is broad and noisy.

content.value_statement: Swift Concurrency changes can create confusing warnings, data-race risks, and migration delays. This skill gives structured guidance for async/await, actors, Sendable, testing, performance, and Swift 6 adoption.
actual_capabilities: Explains async/await, async let, task groups, tasks, and AsyncSequence patterns.
Guides actor isolation, @MainActor usage, custom actors, and nonisolated access decisions.
Helps plan Swift 6 strict concurrency migrations with smaller reviewable changes.
Reviews Sendable warnings and documents safe alternatives to risky escape hatches.
Covers Core Data, testing, memory management, threading, and performance considerations.
Provides decision trees, troubleshooting paths, and verification checklists.
limitations: It does not run Xcode, compile Swift code, or execute tests by itself.
Migration advice depends on project settings, Swift language mode, and toolchain version.
Examples are educational and must be adapted to each codebase.
External course links and package references may change outside the skill.

use_cases.title: Resolve Concurrency Diagnostics
use_cases.description: Diagnose Swift compiler warnings about actor isolation, Sendable, @MainActor, and async boundaries.

use_cases.title: Plan Swift 6 Migration
use_cases.description: Create a staged migration plan that limits blast radius and includes verification steps.

use_cases.title: Stabilize Async Tests
use_cases.description: Improve XCTest or Swift Testing coverage for async code, cancellation, actors, and task groups.

prompt_templates.title: Explain a Warning
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain this Swift Concurrency warning in plain language. Identify the isolation boundary and suggest the smallest safe fix.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Callback Code
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this closure-based Swift API and propose an async/await version. Include cancellation, error handling, and testing notes.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Actor Isolation
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this actor and @MainActor usage. Point out reentrancy risks, unnecessary actor hops, and safer isolation choices.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Swift 6 Migration Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a Swift 6 strict concurrency migration plan for this module. Include settings to check, rollout order, escape-hatch rules, and verification steps.

output_examples.input: A view model has @MainActor errors after adding async loading.
output_examples.output: The response identifies whether the type should be main-actor isolated, explains the boundary crossing, and suggests a focused fix with test steps.

output_examples.input: A Core Data model triggers Sendable warnings during Swift 6 migration.
output_examples.output: The response recommends passing object IDs across boundaries, using context perform methods, and avoiding unchecked Sendable on managed objects.

output_examples.input: Async tests are flaky after using task groups.
output_examples.output: The response reviews task completion, cancellation, error propagation, deterministic scheduling options, and assertion placement.

best_practices: Confirm Swift version, strict concurrency settings, and default actor isolation before giving migration advice.
Prefer structured concurrency and explain any use of unstructured tasks or escape hatches.
Pair concurrency refactors with focused tests, cancellation checks, and performance verification when relevant.
anti_patterns: Do not apply @MainActor everywhere without proving the code belongs on the main actor.
Do not hide Sendable problems with @unchecked Sendable unless a safety invariant is documented.
Do not block async contexts with semaphores, sleeps, or synchronous waits.

faq.question: Does this skill write Swift code?
faq.answer: Yes. It can propose Swift examples and refactors, but the user must compile and test them.

faq.question: Can it migrate a whole app to Swift 6 automatically?
faq.answer: No. It helps plan and review migrations, but full app migration needs project-specific validation.

faq.question: Does it recommend @MainActor for every warning?
faq.answer: No. The guidance asks for the isolation boundary first and warns against blanket @MainActor fixes.

faq.question: Can it help with Core Data concurrency?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers managed object sendability, context perform patterns, object IDs, and custom executor tradeoffs.

faq.question: Does it cover testing async code?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Swift Testing, XCTest, task groups, cancellation, actor testing, and flaky test patterns.

faq.question: Does it need network access?
faq.answer: No. The skill contains external links and network examples, but it does not need network access to operate.
